summary.brief: Guthier v. DiPreta. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: On December 3, 2018, after years of conservative treatment for plaintiff's right foot and ankle pain, DiPreta operated on plaintiff once again.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff received a dose of an antibiotic prior to surgery and a second dose within 24 hours following the surgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On December 13, 2018, plaintiff had her first postoperative appointment, during which DiPreta examined her and documented that her incisions were healing and showed no signs of infection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff was admitted to the hospital on December 26, 2018, for the treatment of an infection at that incision site.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Flemister opined that, to a reasonable degree of medical certainty, DiPreta complied with the standard of care throughout his postoperative treatment, and that his actions did not cause plaintiff's infection.
evidence_index.speaker: Defense expert

evidence_index.text: Snyder described that, although antibiotics are often provided prophylactically prior to surgery, antibiotics may be provided postoperatively as a prophylactic measure based upon the surgeon's 'preference and judgment,' but are generally not indicated.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiff's expert

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, we find that Supreme Court erred in denying defendants' motion for summary judgment dismissing the complaint.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: Snyder's affidavit was insufficient in raising a triable issue of fact on the issue of causation.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient has a history of conservative treatment for right foot and ankle pain and underwent surgery on December 3, 2018.
past_medical_history: Multiple procedures including left foot and ankle surgery and two total knee replacements

focused_exam.foot: Incision on the outside of the foot displayed signs of maceration on December 21, 2018

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Postoperative care following foot and ankle surgery
critical_findings: Signs of maceration at incision site

requestables.name: Wound Culture
requestables.description: Culture of the wound to check for infection

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents for surgery after conservative treatment

key_events.event: Hospital admission for infection on December 26, 2018

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with signs of maceration at incision site
decision_points.prompt: What should be done to investigate the signs of infection?

options.description: Order wound culture and blood studies
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to confirm infection and guide treatment
Based on visual assessment of maceration

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant prescribed Bactrim without further testing
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant believed it was appropriate based on visual assessment

expected_action_court.description: Wound culture and blood studies should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence suggested further investigation was necessary

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order further testing did not align with standard care
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Further testing would have confirmed infection and guided treatment

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed an infection requiring hospitalization and additional treatments

key_findings: Plaintiff's expert affidavit failed to raise a question of fact with respect to causation

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Further testing could have confirmed infection earlier

court_findings.finding: Plaintiff's expert affidavit failed to raise a question of fact with respect to causation
